reply_content: Exposing the business is what ruined it, num nuts. I was in it. Back in the late 90s and early 2000s. It was the same with Magic, when that masked illusionist exposed the tricks, everyone stopped watching; some things are better left to the imagination than being exposed to a bunch of clowns that have never watched it or made fun of it. reply_content: @timeknowsfilmsproductions978 No, Vince McMahon exposed the business in court in 94 to beat the steroid case. He pulled the Montreal Screwjob on live television, allowed the Curtain Call, and broke kayfabe in his declaration for the change to the Attitude Era in 97. And then WWF had the most prosperous era of its entire history. One NBC special exposing the secrets of wrestling didn’t ruin wrestling. What ruined wrestling was losing the 2 biggest stars of the attitude era within a year, the WCW buyout that has left WWE without viable competition to this day, and Vince McMahon getting complacent with the product and placating to shareholders and relying on TV rights deals more than the fans. Among a multitude of other reasons. You know where WWF was before McMahon “exposed the business”, heading in to one of the worst times in the company’s history and them almost shutting down and losing to WCW. Correlation doesn’t equal causation. The exposure of the business was happening due to the rise of the internet and it just wasn’t worth holding dear to the facade. So guess what numb nuts? I don’t really care if you were “in it” because I was, too. The fact of the matter is, we live in a different time and this content appeals to audiences and if WWE wants to put this content out then more power to them and the people that want to watch will, and those that don’t won’t watch. Just like the McMahon documentary that exposed 40 years of history, this is for the casual observers of wrestling that aren’t as fully invested as clearly either of us are.
